questions.text: I experience the vulnerability of sharing something personal as a full-body trembling, not just nervousness.
I think of time as a finite resource that should be allocated thoughtfully.
I learn what I want to achieve by starting to act, not by planning in advance.
I believe that suffering creates a moral obligation that is more compelling than any argument against acting.
I believe the best conversations are those where everyone speaks plainly.
I feel most alive when I am working toward my own aspirations.
I can handle multiple stressors simultaneously without feeling destabilized.
I feel that each day I do not contribute to someone else's well-being is a day partially wasted.
Even minor stressors can set off a cascade of intense internal sensations.
I find that the felt weight of suffering is the experience that most consistently reminds me that my ethical responsibilities extend beyond my immediate circle.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the consideration that should discipline every exercise of power, whether personal, institutional, or political.
I notice when two people have developed private shorthand or inside references that exclude others in the group.
I feel that the most important line item in my budget is what I give away.
I reschedule plans without guilt when something more important or interesting comes up.
I rehearse new procedures mentally before executing them for the first time.
I think that effective leadership requires the ability to make decisions about people's lives without being swayed by their individual stories.
I tend to view others' distress through a lens of objective evaluation rather than personal resonance.
I am attuned to the way social norms shift from one context to another within the same group.
I am drawn to a close-knit crew that steers the ship together.
I plan my errands in an efficient route rather than running them randomly.
I routinely turn down invitations that do not serve my personal interests or growth.
I routinely put my health second to the needs of those who depend on me.
I prefer to receive tasks from a coordinator rather than assign them.
When I see an opportunity, I move quickly to claim it.
I am happy to pass the microphone to whoever wants it.
I tend to redirect conversations toward topics that relate to my own goals.
I hold the view that maintaining rational composure during painful events is not a sign of disengagement but of deep respect for the stakes involved.
I rarely register when someone raises their eyebrows briefly to acknowledge a shared understanding.
I experience the moment before opening important results — test scores, emails, messages — as near-paralyzing tension.
My internal state resembles a deep, slow river — there is movement, but it is steady and unhurried.
I hold the view that emotional events are data points, and I process them without being swept away.
I quickly claim the most strategic position in any group effort.
I am attuned to the silent negotiations that happen before a group makes a decision.
I quickly identify who should do what and communicate it directly.
I do not naturally sense when someone wants to be contradicted rather than agreed with.
I think of each day as its own self-contained experience rather than an investment in the future.
